Keywords: overview, tldr, summary, tl;dr, intro, background, context
Purpose: Gives any reader a 2-3 sentence understanding of what is being built and why. Frames all that follows.
Template:
## Overview
**What:** [One sentence on what we are building]
**Why:** [Business problem and user pain in one sentence]
**Impact:** [Expected business outcome — quantified if possible]
Writing Tips: Write this last. Distill the entire PRD into 3 sentences. Include expected ARR impact or user metric. If you can't summarize it in 3 sentences, the scope may be too large.
Common Mistakes: Too long (>1 paragraph), no numbers, written as a feature list instead of a value statement

Keywords: problem, pain, why, opportunity, background, context, evidence, research
Purpose: Describes the current state and why it is broken from the user's perspective. Grounds the team in the real pain before any solution is proposed.
Template:
## Problem Statement
**Current state:** [What is happening today and why it fails users]
**Evidence:** [Data: user research quotes, support ticket volume, churn attribution, usage data]
**User impact:** [How users currently work around this problem]
**Business impact:** [Churn, lost deals, support load, opportunity cost]
**Why now:** [Why solving this is the right priority today]
Writing Tips: Lead with a story from a real user if possible. Quantify everything: '10% of users', '$450K in lost deals', '40% drop-off'. Distinguish between user pain and business pain — both matter.
Common Mistakes: Describing the problem as a feature gap ('our app doesn't have X') instead of user pain. No data. No quantification. Skipping user impact. Writing the solution in this section.

Keywords: persona, user, audience, who, segment, buyer, customer
Purpose: Defines exactly who this is being built for. Prevents building for a fictional average user that satisfies no one.
Template:
## Target Users
| Persona | Description | Job to Be Done | % of Users |
|---------|-------------|----------------|------------|
| [Primary persona] | [Role, context] | [Core need] | [~X%] |
| [Secondary persona] | [Role, context] | [Core need] | [~X%] |

**Out of scope users:** [Who we are explicitly NOT building for]
Writing Tips: Use real job titles from your CRM or user research. Name the persona concisely. Add the percentage if known — it forces prioritization. List out-of-scope personas to prevent design-by-committee.
Common Mistakes: Using generic 'user' or 'customer'. Not listing out-of-scope users. Confusing buyer persona with end-user persona. Missing the economic buyer for B2B products.

Keywords: goal, objective, okr, strategy, why, purpose, north star, initiative
Purpose: Connects the feature to company strategy. Answers 'why should we build this now instead of something else?'
Template:
## Goals
**Company OKR:** [Which company-level objective this supports]
**Product Goal:** [The specific product outcome being targeted]
**Strategic fit:** [Why this is the right solution direction]
**Why now:** [Market timing, competitive pressure, or dependency that makes this urgent]
Writing Tips: Link explicitly to a named company OKR. If you can't trace this to an OKR, the prioritization may be wrong. Keep this section separate from metrics — goals are directional, metrics are measurable.
Common Mistakes: Listing features as goals. Writing goals that are really metrics ('increase DAU by 20%'). Vague goals like 'improve UX'.

Keywords: metric, kpi, measure, goal, success, target, outcome, result, data
Purpose: Defines what 'done' looks like after launch. Creates accountability and enables post-launch evaluation.
Template:
## Success Metrics
| Metric | Type | Baseline | Target | Timeline |
|--------|------|----------|--------|----------|
| [Primary metric] | Primary | [Current] | [Goal] | [Date] |
| [Secondary metric] | Supporting | [Current] | [Goal] | [Date] |
| [Leading indicator] | Leading | [Current] | [Goal] | [Date] |

**Measurement approach:** [How will we track these? Which tool/event?]
Writing Tips: Choose 1 primary metric and 2-3 supporting metrics. Avoid vanity metrics (page views, clicks) unless tied to a business outcome. Define the measurement method alongside the metric, or it won't get implemented.
Common Mistakes: No metrics at all. Vanity metrics ('pageviews') not tied to business outcomes. No baseline. No timeline. No measurement plan. All metrics set as P0.

Keywords: guardrail, safety, protect, regression, retain, don't break, floor, threshold
Purpose: Lists the metrics that must NOT decrease as a side effect of this feature. Protects against unintended regressions.
Template:
## Guardrail Metrics
The following metrics must not drop below current baseline as a result of this feature:
- [Metric 1]: Must not drop below [threshold] (current: [value])
- [Metric 2]: Must not drop below [threshold] (current: [value])
- [Metric 3]: Must not drop below [threshold] (current: [value])
Writing Tips: Think about what this feature could break accidentally. Common guardrails: performance, retention, NPS, error rate. A retention feature should guard against DAU drop. A new onboarding flow should guard against conversion rate.
Common Mistakes: Skipping this section entirely. Only listing aspirational targets and forgetting what could regress.

Keywords: story, user story, as a, i want, so that, use case, scenario
Purpose: Describes what different user types need to accomplish in their own language. Bridges product intent and engineering implementation.
Template:
## User Stories

### US-[N]: [Short story title]
**As a** [specific persona],
**I want** [clear user-centric action],
**so that** [tangible outcome tied to value].

**Priority:** P0 / P1 / P2

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Given [context], when [action], then [outcome]
- [ ] Given [context], when [action], then [outcome]
- [ ] [Edge case or negative scenario handled]
Writing Tips: Use a specific persona, not 'as a user'. Describe the goal from the user's perspective, not a UI action. Always include the 'so that' clause — it's the business justification. Mark each story with a priority. Write AC before handing off to engineering.
Common Mistakes: Generic 'As a user' persona. Missing 'so that' clause. Stories that are really epics (split them). Stories that prescribe UI implementation. No acceptance criteria. All stories marked P0.

Keywords: functional, requirement, feature, behavior, spec, fr, must, should, system
Purpose: Itemizes what the system must do in testable, numbered statements. The reference point for QA and engineering.
Template:
## Functional Requirements
| ID | Requirement | Priority | Notes |
|----|-------------|----------|-------|
| FR-1 | [Testable system behavior] | Must Have | [Context or constraint] |
| FR-2 | [Testable system behavior] | Should Have | [Context or constraint] |
| FR-3 | [Testable system behavior] | Nice to Have | [Context or constraint] |
Writing Tips: Number each requirement for easy reference. Use 'must', 'should', 'may' (RFC 2119 language) to distinguish priority. Write from the system's perspective: 'The system must...' Keep requirements testable — if QA can't verify it, rewrite it.
Common Mistakes: Numbering requirements but making them untestable. Mixing requirements and design notes. Having no must/should/nice-to-have distinction. Writing implementation details instead of behaviors.

Keywords: non-functional, nfr, performance, security, accessibility, reliability, scalability, latency, sla
Purpose: Defines how the system must behave beyond features: speed, security, uptime, accessibility, compliance.
Template:
## Non-Functional Requirements
- **Performance:** [e.g., API response < 200ms p95, page load < 2s on 4G]
- **Reliability:** [e.g., 99.9% uptime, retry on failure]
- **Scalability:** [e.g., support 10,000 concurrent users]
- **Security:** [e.g., all PII encrypted at rest and in transit, RBAC enforced]
- **Accessibility:** [e.g., WCAG 2.1 AA, screen reader compatible]
- **Compliance:** [e.g., GDPR, SOC 2, HIPAA, PCI-DSS]
- **Localization:** [Languages, timezones, currency formats]
Writing Tips: Be specific: 'fast' is not a requirement. '< 200ms p95' is. Include the measurement method. For security, name the specific standard or control required.
Common Mistakes: Writing 'the system should be fast'. No specifics. Missing accessibility entirely. Treating compliance as an afterthought.

Keywords: scope, out of scope, exclusion, not included, future, phase 2, won't have, cut
Purpose: Explicitly lists what this PRD does NOT cover. The most underrated section — prevents scope creep and sets clear expectations.
Template:
## Out of Scope
> The following are explicitly NOT included in this release. Including them is considered scope creep.

| Item | Reason | Future? |
|------|--------|---------|
| [Feature or capability] | [Why excluded] | Phase 2 / No plan |
| [Feature or capability] | [Why excluded] | Phase 2 / No plan |
Writing Tips: Write this before you write the requirements — it forces you to make hard decisions. List anything that came up in discussions and was deferred. Reference separate PRDs for deferred items where they exist.
Common Mistakes: Skipping this section entirely. Listing vague items like 'advanced features'. Not explaining why items are excluded.

Keywords: design, ux, ui, wireframe, mockup, figma, prototype, flow, interaction, accessibility
Purpose: Describes user flows and interaction patterns. Includes links to design files and callouts for key states.
Template:
## Design & User Experience
**Figma link:** [URL]

### User Flow
[Start] → [Step 1] → [Decision Point] → [Outcome A / Outcome B]

### Key Screens
1. **[Screen name]:** [Brief description of what it shows and user goal]
2. **[Screen name]:** [Brief description]

### Key States
| State | Behavior |
|-------|----------|
| Loading | [Skeleton or spinner, behavior] |
| Empty | [Zero-state message and CTA] |
| Error | [Error message and recovery path] |
| Success | [Confirmation and next step] |
Writing Tips: Describe what users need to accomplish on each screen, not just what the screen looks like. Specify the empty, loading, error, and success states explicitly — these are the states most likely to be missed in development.
Common Mistakes: Only linking to Figma with no description. Missing empty/error/loading states. Over-specifying visual design details that should belong in the design file.

Keywords: edge case, error, exception, negative, failure, boundary, corner case, what if
Purpose: Documents scenarios beyond the happy path. The single biggest source of production bugs when skipped.
Template:
## Edge Cases & Error Scenarios
| Scenario | User Action | System Response | Error Message |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|---------------|
| [What can go wrong] | [What user did] | [How system handles it] | [Exact message shown] |
| [Boundary condition] | [What user did] | [How system handles it] | [Exact message shown] |
Writing Tips: For every feature, ask: What if the input is invalid? What if the network fails? What if the user navigates away mid-flow? What if data is missing or in an unexpected format? What if this is a first-time user with no data? What if the user lacks permission?
Common Mistakes: Only documenting the happy path. Vague error handling like 'show an error'. Not specifying the exact error message. Ignoring partial failures.

Keywords: technical, architecture, api, data model, dependency, constraint, performance, security, infrastructure
Purpose: Captures technical context the PM knows that engineering needs. Not a technical spec — that belongs in an engineering design doc.
Template:
## Technical Considerations
**Dependencies:** [Other services, teams, or features this relies on]
**Data requirements:** [New data to store, PII classification, retention policy]
**API changes:** [New or modified endpoints]
**Performance constraints:** [Load profile, latency requirements]
**Security requirements:** [Authentication, authorization, encryption]
**Third-party integrations:** [External services involved]
**Known constraints:** [Platform, legacy system, or infra limitations]
Writing Tips: Write this section WITH an engineering lead, not in isolation. Capture what the PM knows about constraints, but leave architecture decisions to engineering. Flag unknowns as open questions.
Common Mistakes: Writing an engineering design doc inside the PRD. Leaving this section blank and hoping engineering figures it out. Missing compliance or security requirements.

Keywords: dependency, depends, blocked, blocker, team, service, upstream, downstream
Purpose: Lists other teams, services, or work items this PRD is waiting on or that are waiting on it.
Template:
## Dependencies
| Dependency | Owner | Status | Risk | Impact if Delayed |
|------------|-------|--------|------|------------------|
| [Service or team] | [Owner] | [Status] | [H/M/L] | [What breaks] |
Writing Tips: Identify dependencies early — they are the #1 source of missed launch dates. Flag high-risk dependencies explicitly and discuss mitigation with stakeholders before finalizing the PRD.
Common Mistakes: Skipping this section. Not flagging risks on critical dependencies.

Keywords: launch, rollout, release, phased, beta, pilot, feature flag, ga, gradual, communication
Purpose: Describes how the feature will be released safely and progressively.
Template:
## Launch & Rollout Plan
| Phase | Description | Audience | Success Criteria |
|-------|-------------|----------|------------------|
| 1. Internal | Internal dogfooding | Employees | No P0/P1 bugs |
| 2. Beta | Limited release | [N% or segment] | [Metric target] |
| 3. GA | Full release | All users | [Metric target] |

**Feature flags:** [Flag name, rollout percentage plan]
**Rollback plan:** [What triggers a rollback, and exact steps to revert]
**Communication plan:** [When does CS, Sales, Support get notified?]
Writing Tips: Define rollback conditions before launch day — not after. A phased rollout without success criteria for each phase is just hope. Include the internal communication plan so support isn't surprised.
Common Mistakes: No rollback plan. No success criteria per phase. Not including a communication plan for CS and Support.

Keywords: risk, mitigation, assumption, unknown, concern, failure mode, dependency risk
Purpose: Identifies what could go wrong and how to reduce those risks before they materialize.
Template:
## Risks & Mitigations
| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| [What could go wrong] | H/M/L | H/M/L | [How we reduce this risk] |
| [Technical unknown] | H/M/L | H/M/L | [Spike or prototype planned] |
Writing Tips: Think about technical risks (unknown feasibility), adoption risks (users won't use it), compliance risks (legal or regulatory), and dependency risks (blocked by another team). A risk with no mitigation is just a worry — add an action.
Common Mistakes: Only listing risks without mitigations. Skipping this section for 'obvious' features. Not revisiting risks during development.

Keywords: question, decision, unknown, open, tbd, to be determined, log, history
Purpose: Tracks unresolved questions and key decisions made. Shows intellectual honesty and protects against revisiting closed decisions.
Template:
## Open Questions
| Question | Owner | Due Date | Status |
|----------|-------|----------|--------|
| [Unresolved question] | [Who answers it] | [By when] | Open |

## Decision Log
| Date | Decision | Rationale | Alternatives Considered |
|------|----------|-----------|-------------------------|
| [Date] | [What was decided] | [Why] | [What else was considered] |
Writing Tips: Keep open questions visible and assigned — don't bury them. Capture decisions as they're made so the team doesn't re-litigate settled questions three months later.
Common Mistakes: Deleting resolved questions instead of logging them. Not assigning an owner to each open question. Making decisions verbally without documenting them.

Keywords: appendix, reference, link, research, competitor, doc, related, resource
Purpose: Provides links to supporting materials without bloating the main PRD body.
Template:
## Appendix
- User research: [link]
- Competitive analysis: [link]
- Analytics deep-dive: [link]
- Related PRDs: [link]
- Engineering design doc: [link]
- Support ticket analysis: [link]

## Changelog
| Date | Author | Changes |
|------|--------|---------|
| [Date] | [Name] | Initial draft |
Writing Tips: Move detailed supporting evidence here to keep the PRD scannable. Always include a changelog so readers can see what changed and when.
Common Mistakes: Bloating the main PRD with full research docs. Not maintaining the changelog as requirements evolve.
